text: CLARE, a district of Nova Scotia, in the co. of Annapolis, extending along the S side of St. Mary's bay towards Yarmouth. It is a comparatively fertile district, and is inhabited by about 4,500 Acadians, or colonists of French descent.

CLARE, or CLARA, an island in the p. of Kilgever, co. Mayo, in the middle of the entrance of Clew bay, about 21 m. SSW of Achilbeg, and 141 m. W of Westport. Length, from east to west, 41 m.; breadth, 2 m. Area 3,959 acres, 21 perches. The NW side rises to the elevation of several hundred feet, and presents to the ocean a fine mural face of precipitous cliffs; but the SW side, though facing the whole breadth of the Atlantic, shelves down to a comparatively low and even tame sea-board. The highest ground is Knockmore, on the W coast. Altitude 1,520 ft. above sea-level. A lighthouse crowns a lofty cliff on the NE extremity; and forms a good mark for entering Clew bay. Good anchorage is obtained, in SW winds, under the lee of the island. At the principal creek, at the E end of the island, are a rather populous village, and a picturesquely situated old castle. Pop. in 1831, 1,616.
CLARE, or CLARE-MORRIS, a town in the p. of Kilcoleman, co. Mayo, 5 m. NNE of Hollymount. Pop. in 1831, 1,476; in 1841, 2,256.
CLARE-ABBEY, a parish in co. Clare. Area 7,028 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,881; in 1841, 3,280. Houses 516. It contains the town of Clare.
CLAREBOROUGH, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 3 m. NE of East Retford. Area 3,870 acres. Pop. 2,207.
CLARE-GALWAY, a parish, 61 m. NE by N of Galway, co. Galway. Area 12,451 acres. Pop. 2,586.

CLAREMONT, a township in Sullivan co., in New Hampshire, U. S., on Connecticut river. Pop. 3,217. -Also a v. in Picken's district, in South Carolina, 162 m. NW of Columbia.—Also a township in Erie co., in the state of New York, U. S., 270 m. W of Albany. Pop. 2,271.—Also a v. of Upper Canada, in Burford township, 9 m. from Brantford. Pop. 150.

CLAREMONT ISLES, a group of five small islets of coral formation, off the NE coast of Australia, between S lat. 13° 29' and 13° 56', and E long. 143° 33', and 143° 40\frac{1}{2}'. They are covered with small brush-wood, and are from 6 to 7 m. apart.

CLARENCE, a township of Upper Canada, in the Ottawa district, bounded by the Ottawa river on the N, and intersected by a branch of the Petite Nation river. About 800 acres of the surface are under cultivation. Pop. 200.

CLARENCE COVE, a bay and settlement at the N end of the island of Fernando Po, between Goderich bay on the E, and Cockburn cove on the W. There is good anchorage here, with a bold beach and good landing. The bay abounds with fish and turtle. The establishment here has been formed with a view to putting down the slave trade, and comprises the governor's house, the house of the commission for the adjudication of captured slave vessels, a large hospital, and marine barracks. The Kroomen and freed Negroes, about 2,000 in number, have a collection of small neat huts near the government-house, with small CLA gardens in front and rear of each cottage, in which they cultivate Indian corn, bananas, and pepper.

CLARENCE ISLAND, one of the three large islands composing Tierra-del-Fuego, the extremity of the S American continent. It lies to the S of Brunswick peninsula, between 53° 48' and 54° 20' S lat., and 71° and 72° 20' W long.; and is 52 m. in length, and 23 m. broad.

CLARENCE LAKE, or GREAT LAKE, a lake in Van Diemen's Land, in the S part of the division of Norfolk Plains. It is of irregular outline, and its circuit, following the deep indentations of its numerous bays, has been estimated at from 75 m. to 150 m. in extent. It discharges its waters by the Shannon, which emerges a full-grown river from its SE extremity, and flows SE and S to join the Ouse, a great tributary of the Derwent. Its scenery is reported to be very beautiful; but its elevation is such that cereal cultivation is said to be impracticable in its environs, the sharp summer frosts nipping the young plants long ere they can reach a point of safety. The adjacent plains and marshes, however, afford good pasturage for sheep. An extraordinary feature of this lake is a species of natural breakwater that protects its lee-shore, presenting a regular causeway with "joints almost as perfect as though arranged by rule and scale."

CLARENCE (Mount), a granitic hill of Western Australia, on the coast of King George's sound, in the co. of Plantagenet.—Also a ridge forming part of the enclosing mountains of the vale of Clywd in New South Wales.
CLARENCE PLAINS, a large peninsular projection on the S coast of the district of Richmond in Van Diemen's Land, forming the NW side of Ralph bay, and bounded by the estuary of the Derwent on the W.

CLARENCE (PORT), a spacious harbour on the S coast of Behring's straits, in Russian America, in N lat. 65° 16′ 40″, W long. 166° 47′ 50″. It enters between Points Jackson and Spencer; and consists of an outer and inner harbour. The outer harbour, formed by a low spit of land projecting about 10 m. from the coast, and having a channel about 2 m. wide between its extremity and the N shore, is 9 m. broad, and has a depth of 51 fath. The inner is 10 m. in length, by <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. in breadth, with a depth of from 21 to 3 fath. The adjacent country is swampy and full of ruts, and the soil is covered with a thick coating of moss, while the valleys and hollows are filled with dwarf willow and birch.—Beechey.

CLARENCE STRAIT, an outlet to sea of Van Diemen's gulf on the N coast of Australia. It separates Bathurst and Melville islands from the mainland; and is 75 m. in length, and from 17 to 35 m. wide. It is narrowest near its centre, between capes Gambier and Eldon; and in this part is a group of four low rocky islands covered with mangroves, called Vernon's islands. Flood-tide sets to E through this channel into the gulf, and the strait "is navigable with common precaution." [Stokes.]—Also a channel in the Persian gulf, separating the island of Kishm from the mainland of Persia. It is 13 m. across at its greatest width, but contracts in the middle of its length to 3 m. It is studded with islets.— Also a narrow channel on the coast of Russian America, separating Prince of Wales island on the W, from Duke of York's island on the E.
CLARENDON PARK, a liberty and hamlet in Wilts, 3 m. E by S of Salisbury. The park, the area of which is nearly 4,300 acres, was anciently a royal forest and chase. Pop. 183.

CLARENDON, a township in Rutland co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 70 m. SSW of Montpelier, intersected by Otter creek. Pop. 1,549.-Also a township in Orleans co., in the state of New York, 253 m. W by N of Albany, intersected by branches of Sandy and Oak-Orchard creeks. Pop. 2,251.- Also a township in Calhoun co., in the state of Michigan, 101 m. W of Detroit. Pop. 506.-Also a v. in Monroe co., in Arkansas, 60 m. E of Little Rock.

CLARENS, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 3 m. ESE of Vevay, on the lake of Geneva. It has been written into notice by Rousseau; but M. Simond affirms that it is "a dirty village, less prettily situated than any in the neighbourhood," and chosen by the author of the 'Nouvelle-Héloise,' "for no better reason than a well-sounding name,"-an opinion in which Murray's Hand-book coincides, maugre Byron's passionate address to "Clarens, sweet Clarens, birth-place of deep Love;" and his grave attestation, that "if Rousseau had never written nor lived, the same associations would not less have belonged to such scenes."
CLARENSAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Saint-Mamert. Pop. 1,090.
CLARE'NZA. See GLARENTZA.
CLARIDON, a township in Marion co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. 1,084.—Also a township in Geauga co., Ohio, 30 m. E of Cleveland, on the head-branches of the Cuyahoga river. Pop. 897.

CLARION, a county in the NW part of the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., bounded by the Alleghany river on the SW, and intersected by the Clarion river. Area 530 sq. m.—Its cap., of the same name, is 184 m. WNW of Harrisburg. Pop. of the township 2,239.—Also a river rising in M'Kean co., in the state of Pennsylvania, and flowing 70 m. SW to the Alleghany. It is navigable for boats 55 m.

CLARION ISLAND, an island in the Pacific, in N lat. 18° 24′, W long. 114° 33′. It is about 8 m. in length from E to W, and rises high towards its W end. It is occasionally visited by American whalers, and abounds with turtle.
CLARITZA. See KLARITZA.

CLARK, a village in Mercer co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 245 m. WNW of Harrisburg.— Also a township in Lincoln co., in Missouri. Pop. 1,218.—Also a township in Cole co., in Missouri. Pop. 853.—Also a township in Gasconade co., in Missouri. Pop. 621.—Also a township in Johnson co., in Arkansas. Pop. 401.—Also a township in Pope co., in Arkansas. Pop. 296.—Also a township in Brown co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,290 .- Also a township in Clinton co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,297.—Also a township in Coshocton co., in Ohio. Pop. 703.—Also a township in Montgomery co., in Iowa. Pop. 603.

CLARKE, a county in the NE part of the state of Virginia, U.S., intersected by the Shenandoah. Area 225 sq. m. Pop. 6,353. Its cap. is Berryville.—Also a co. centrally situated in the N part of the state of Georgia, and intersected by Oconee river. Area 414 sq. m. Pop. 10,522. Its cap. is Watkinsville.—Also a co. in Alabama, in the SW part of the state, between the Tombigbee and Alabama rivers. Area 1,200 sq. m. Pop. 8,640. Its cap. is Macon.—Also a co. in Mississippi, towards the E border of the state, intersected by the Chickasawha. Area 650 sq. m. Pop. 2,986. Its cap. is Quitman.—Also a co. in the E part of Kentucky, to the N of Kentucky river. Area 300 sq. m. Pop. 10,802. Its cap. is Winchester.-Also a co. toward the SW part of the state of Ohio, intersected by Mad river, and Beaver and Buck creeks. Area 412 sq. m. Pop. 16,882. Its cap. is Springfield.—Also a co. in the SE part of Iowa, on the Ohio river. Area 400 sq. m. Pop. 14,595. Its cap. is Charleston.—Also a co. in the E part of Illinois, intersected by Crane and Fox creeks. Area CLA 580 part of Missouri, having the Mississippi on the E, and the Des Moines on the NE. Area 560 sq. m. Pop. 2,846. Its cap. is Waterloo.—Also a co. in the S part of Arkansas, on the Wachitta river. Pop. 2,309. Its cap. is Greenville.
CLARKE RIVER, a large river of Northern Australia, forming the SW branch of the Burdekin. Near its junction with the latter, it flows through a fine broad openly timbered valley.
CLARKE'S FORK, a river of North America, rising in the Rocky mountains, and falling into Yellowstone river, one of the tributaries of the Missouri, 100 m. above the mouth of the Bighorn.

CLARKE'S ISLAND, the southernmost of the range which bears the name of Furneaux, in Bass' strait. It is 63 m. in length from ENE to WSW, and 4½ m. wide. Its S end is in S lat. 40° 33', E long. 148° 9½'.—Flinders.

CLARKE'S RIVER, or FLATHEAD, a river of Oregon, which takes its rise in the Rocky mountains near the sources of the Missouri; runs NW, and unites with the Columbia 15 m. NE of Fort Colville. It falls into the Columbia over a confused heap of large rocks, at a point 2,200 ft. above sealevel, with as large a body of water as that of the Columbia itself above the junction. In its course through a rich valley, surrounded by lofty snow-clad mountains, it expands into Kulleespelm lake, 35 m. in length, and from 5 to 8 m. in breadth; and 50 m. above this lake, it is formed by two forks, of which the S rises near the Grand defile in the Rocky mountains, and the N takes its rise in Flathead lake.

CLARK-HILL, a mountain rising behind Old Fort Norman, on the Mackenzie river, in the northern part of the American continent. It is visible from Fort Franklin on the N shore of Keith bay, bearing S, and forming part of a ridge about 50 m. distant; and its alt. may be guessed at 1,500 ft. above the Mackenzie.
CLARKSBOROUGH, a village in Gloucester co., in New Jersey, U. S., 41 m. SW of Trenton.

CLARKSBURG, a village in Montgomery co., in the state of Maryland, 68 m. WNW of Annapolis.— Also the cap. of Harrison co., in Virginia, on the Monongahela river, 253 m. NW of Richmond.— Also the cap. of Lewis co., in Kentucky, 107 m. ENE of Frankfort, on Salt-Lick creek.—Also a township in Berkshire co., in the state of Massachusetts, 125 m. W by N of Boston. Pop. 370.
CLARKSFIELD, a township of Huron co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 102 m. NE of Columbus, on Vermilion river, 16 m. above its mouth. Pop. in 1840, 1,473.

CLARKSON, a township of Monroe co., in the state of New York, U.S., 236 m. NW of Albany, and 16 m. NW of Rochester, bounded on the N by Lake Ontario, and drained by its tributaries, Little Salmon and Sandy creeks. Its surface is level, and it consists of sand and gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 3,486. The village is on the Ridgeroad, and contained at the same period about 700 inhabitants.- Also a village of Middleton township, Columbia co., in the state of Ohio, 159 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 63.

CLARKSTOWN, a township in Rochland co., in the state of New York, U. S., 122 m. S of Albany, bounded on the E by Hudson river, and containing Rochland lake, noted for its ice, with which the market of New York is supplied. The surface is hilly, but the valleys are generally fertile. Pop. 2,533.

CLARKSVILLE, a village of New Scotland township, Albany co., in the state of New York, U. S., 14 m. SW of Albany.—Also a township of Coos co., in the state of New Hampshire, on the E side of Connecticut river, near its source. Pop. 88.-Also a v. of Lebanon township, Hunterdon co., in the state of 1,080 sq. m. Pop. 7,453.—Also a co. in the NE | New Jersey, 45 m. NW of Trenton, on Spruce run. Iron and plumbago are found in the vicinity.—Also a township of Alleghany co., in the state of New York. Pop. 326.—Also a village of Greene co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 203 m. WSW of Harrisburg, at the junction of the N and S Forks of Ten Mile creek.—Also a v., the cap. of Habersham co., in the state of Georgia, 138 m. N of Milledgeville. Pop. 300.—Also a v., cap. of Clarke co., in the state of Alabama, 134 m. S of Tuscaloosa.—Also a v., cap. of Montgomery co., in the state of Tennessee, 45 m. NW of Nashville, at the junction of Red and Cumberland rivers.—Also a v. of Vernon township, Clinton co., in the state of Ohio, 82 m. SW of Columbus. -Also a v. of Pike co., in the state of Missouri, 99 m. NE of Jefferson city, on the W side of the Mississippi.—Also a v. in the township of Camden, in Upper Canada, on the Napanee river, 2 m. above Newburgh. Pop. 200.

CLARO, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, district of Riviera, in the Val Laventina, on the l. bank of the Tessin, 4 m. N of Bellinzona. Pop. 1,087. It contains a Benedictine convent, founded in 1,275.

CLARO, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto- Grosso, which runs W, and unites with the Rio-Porrudos or São-Lourenco, 70 m. NE of the confluence of that river with the Paraguay.—Also a river in the prov. of Goyaz, which descends from the Serra-de- Santa - Martha; receives the auriferous streams of Fartura and Piloes; and, after a total course of 120 m. in a generally NNW direction, falls into the Araguaia. Diamonds have been found in this river.— Also a river of Chili, in the prov. of Maule, which descends from the Andes and unites with the Maule, 10 m. W of Talca. See also Rio Claro.

CLARRET, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Herault, arrond. of Montpellier. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 1,984; in 1841, 2,001.—The village is 19 m. N of Montpellier, in a valley surrounded by arid rocks. Pop. in 1846, 756. The mulberry is cultivated in the environs.
CLARY, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of the Nord, arrond. of Cambrai.
CLASE, a hamlet in the p. of Llangafelach, Glamorganshire, 5 m. NW of Swansea. Pop. 5,924.— Also a township in the p. of St. Harmon. Pop. 275.
CLASHACROW, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 14 m. SSE of Freshford. Area 999 acres. Pop. 291.
CLASHAVODIG, a village in the p. of Little- Island, co. Cork. Pop. 362.
CLASHMORE, a parish and village in co. Waterford. The p. lies along the l. bank of the Blackwater. Area 7,201 acres. Pop. 3,777. The v. is 3 m. SSE of Aglish. Pop. 232.
CLATFORD, a tything in the p. of Pershute, Wilts, 2 m. SW of Marlborough. Pop. 90.
CLATFORD-GOODWORTH, a parish of Hants, 2 m. S of Andover, and intersected by the Andover canal. Area 3,390 acres. Pop. 413.
CLATFORD (UPPER), a parish of Hants, 1 m. S of Andover. Area 970 acres. Pop. 160.
CLATSOP. See ASTORIA (FORT).
CLATT, a parish and village in Aberdeenshire. The p., which is intersected by the Gaudie, had a pop. of 535 in 1831, and 524 in 1841. The v. is 10 m. S of Huntly.
CLATTERCOTE, an extra-parochial district of Oxfordshire, 6 m. N of Banbury, intersected by the Oxford canal. Area 670 acres. Pop. 15.
CLATWORTHY, a parish of Somerset, 3 m. NE of Wiveliscombe. Area 4,000 acres. Pop. 309.

CLAUDE (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Charente, arrond. of CLA Confolens. The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,440; in 1841, 15,032.—The town is on the r. bank of the Son, 14 m. WSW of Confolens. Pop. 1,995. It has some iron-forges, and a monthly fair.

CLAUDE (SAINT), an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Jura. The arrond. comprises an area of 103,899 hect., and the 5 cant. of Bouchoux, Sainte-Claude, Saint-Laurent, Moirans, and Morez. Pop. in 1831, 52,433; in 1846, 52,112.—The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,995; in 1841, 16,740.—The town is situated at the base of the Jura range, at the confluence of the Bienne with the Tacon, 27 m. SE of Lons-le- Saulnier, and 20 m. NW of Geneva. Pop. in 1789, 3,640; in 1821, 4,463; in 1836, 5,238; and in 1846, 5,471. With the exception of the church of Saint Pierre, the college, and some public fountains, it contains no buildings worthy of note. It possesses, however, extensive manufactories of articles in horn, shell, wood, metal, bone, and ivory, and children's toys; and has several tanneries and breweries, a printing-establishment and a large paper-mill, a cotton-spinning and a wire-mill, &c. Fairs are held monthly, the trade of which is very active, and consists in horses, cattle, grain, cheese, timber, mercery, turnery, hardware, and other varieties of articles of local manufacture.- In 1742 Saint C. was erected into a bishopric. The town was almost entirely destroyed by fire in 1799.
CLAUDE-DE-DIRAY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Blois. Pop. 1,515.
CLAUDON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Monthureux-sur-Saône, 22 m. S of Mirecourt. Pop. 1,574.

CLAUGHTON, a parish of Lancashire, 7 m. NE of Lancaster, E of the Lune. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 118.—Also a township in the p. and 2 m. SE of Garstang, in the same co., intersected by the Lancaster and Preston railway. Area 3,300 acres. Pop. 772.
CLAUGHTON-CUM-GRANGE, a township in the p. of Bidstone, Cheshire, 8 m. N of Great Norton. Area 700 acres. Pop. 240.
CLAUSE. See CHIUSA.
CLAUSENBURG. See KLAUSENBURG.
CLAUSHOLM, a village of Denmark, in the prov. of Jutland and bailiwick of Randers.
CLAUSS, a defile and fortress of Austria, on the confines of the prov. of Lower Austria and Styria, near the source of the Steyer.
CLAUSTHAL. See KLAUSTHAL.
CLAVEISOLLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Saint-Nizier-d'Azergue, 16 m. NW of Villefranche, on the Azergue. Pop. 1,226.
CLAVEN. See CHIAVENNA.

CLAVERACK, a township of Columbia co., in the state of New York, U. S., 4 m. E of Hudson, and 34 m. S of Albany, watered by Claverack creek. The surface is hilly, and the soil, consisting of gravelly loam and clay, is generally fertile. Pop. 3,056. The village contains about 300 inhabitants.
CLAVERDON, a parish of Warwickshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. E of Henley-in-Arden, and 5 m. from Warwick, intersected by the Birmingham and Stratford canal. Area 4,330 acres. Pop. 711.
CLAVERHAM, a hamlet in the p. of Yatton, Somerset. Pop. 322.
CLAVERING, a parish of Essex, 61 m. NNW of Stansted-Mountfitchet, on the Stort. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 1,172.
CLAVERLEY, a parish and township of Salop, 5 m. E of Bridgenorth. Area 7,530 acres. Pop. of p. 1,669; of township 404.
CLAVERTON, or CLAVERDON, a parish of Somerset, 21 m. ESE of Bath. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 177.
CLAVIE'RES, a village of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Ruynes, 11 m. ESE of St. Flour. Pop. 900. It has some manufactories of common stuffs.
CLAVIERS, a village of France, in the dep. of the Var, cant. and 11 m. NE of Callas. Pop. 1,119.
CLAVIGO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and partido and 10 m. S of Logrono.
CLAVILLE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. and 7 m. WNW of Evreux. Pop. 180.
CLAWDD-MADOG, a hamlet in the p. of Llanwrtyd, Breconshire, 12 m. W of Builth. Pop. 341.
CLAWRPLWYF, a hamlet in the p. of Mynyddyslwyn, Monmouthshire. Area 5,870 acres. Pop. 2,055.
CLAWTON, a parish of Devon, 3½ m. S of Holsworthy, on the Claw. Area 5,950 acres. Pop. 639.
CLAXBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 3 m. S of Alford. Area 590 acres. Pop. 132.—Also a parish of the same co., 31 m. N of Market-Raisen. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 220.
CLAXBY-PLUCKACRE, a parish of Lincolnshire, 41 m. SE of Horncastle. Area 690 acres. Pop. 29.
CLAXTON, a township in the p. of Greatham, county of Durham, 6 m. NNE of Stockton-upon- Tees. Area 610 acres. Pop. 52.—Also a parish of Norfolk, 7 m. ESE of Norwich. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. 200.
CLAXTON, or Long Clawson, a parish of Leicestershire, 6 m. NNW of Melton-Mowbray. Area 3,450 acres. Pop. 838.
CLAXTON-ON-THE-MOOR, a township partly in the parishes of Bossall and Foston, N. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. NNE of York. Area 880 acres. Pop. 168.

CLAY, a lake in the p. and about a m. S of Keady, co. of Armagh, Ulster. On its bank is the townland of the same name, which forms one of 28 leased by the Board of Trinity college for the produce of lead to the Mining company of Ireland.

CLAY, a county of the state of Kentucky, U. S., comprising an area of 880 sq. m., drained by the SW Fork of Kentucky river. It possesses a light soil, and contains some mineral, coal, and salt-springs. Pop. in 1830, 3,548; in 1840, 4,607, of whom 3,954 were whites, 503 slaves, and 150 free coloured. Its cap. is Manchester. - Also a co. in the state of Indiana, comprising an area of 360 sq. m., watered by Eel river and its branches. It contains much good land, covered to a considerable extent with heavy timber, and in the SW are some fine prairies. Pop. in 1830, 1,616; in 1840, 5,567. Its cap. is Bowlinggreen.—Also a co. in the state of Illinois, comprising an area of 475 sq. m., generally level and fertile, and drained by Little Wabash river. Pop. in 1840, 3,228. Its cap. is Lewisville.—Also a co. in the state and on the N side of the Missouri, comprising an undulating area of 432 sq. m., drained by Fishing and a branch of Little Platte rivers. Pop. in 1830, 5,338; in 1840, 8,282, of whom 6,373 were whites, 1,875 slaves, and 34 free coloured. Its cap. is Liberty. - Also a township of Onondaga co., in the state of New York, 142 m. WNW of Albany, watered by the Oneida and Seneca rivers, which here unite to form the Oswego river. The surface is undulating, and the soil productive. Pop. 2,852.—Also a township of St. Clair co., in the state of Michigan, 62 m. NNE of Detroit. Pop. 387.—Also a township of Lafayette co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. 1,305. -Also a township of Ralls co., in the same state. Pop. 1,049.—Also a township of Tuscarawas co., in the state of Ohio, intersected by the Ohio canal and I CLA 582 Muskingum river, 10 or 15 m. below New Philadelphia. Pop. 864.—Also a township of Scioto co., in the same state, bordered on the W by the Scioto. It is partly hilly, but generally extremely fertile. Pop. 696.—Also a township of Ottawa co., in the same state, on Portage river, 16 m. W of Lower Sandusky. Pop. 176.—Also a township of Montgomery co., in the same state. Pop. 1,633.—Also a township of Highland co., in the same state. Pop. 783.—Also a township of Allen co., in the same state. Pop. 435. -Also a township of Gallia co., in the same state. Pop. 745.—Also a township of Knox co., in the same state. Pop. 1,302.—Also a township of Hamilton co., in the state of Indiana. Pop. 730.— Also a township of La Grange co., in the same state. Pop. 99.—Also a township of Owen co., in the same state. Pop. 804.—Also a township of Wayne co., in the same state. Pop. 923.
CLAYBROOKE, a parish of Leicestershire, 4 m. from Hinckley, and 5 m. from Lutterworth. Area 5,380 acres. Pop. 1,428.

CLAYBROOKE (GREAT and LITTLE), townships of the p. of Claybrooke, Leicestershire. The former is 5 m. NW of Lutterworth. Pop. 519.—The latter is 4 m. WNW of Lutterworth, in the centre of the p. Pop. 104.
CLAY-CASTLE, a small hilly promontory, on the W side of Youghal harbour, about a m. SW of Youghal, co. Cork, Munster.
CLAYCOTON, a parish of Northamptonshire. Area 1,380 acres. Pop. 107.

CLAYDON, a parish of Suffolk, 44 m. NNW of Ipswich, near the London and Norwich railway. Area 600 acres. Pop. 418.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Cropready, Oxfordshire, 62 m. N of Banbury, on the Oxford canal. Area 1,160 acres. Pop. 337.
CLAYDON (EAST), a parish of Bucks, 23 m. SW of Winslow. Area 2,160 acres. Pop. 378.
CLAYDON (MIDDLE), a parish of Bucks, 4 m. SW of Winslow. Area 2,850 acres. Pop. 127.
CLAYDON (STEEPLE), or STEEPLE-CLAYDON, a parish of Bucks, 4½ m. W of Winslow. Area 3,270 acres. Pop. 849.

CLAYE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, arrond. of Meaux. The cant. comprises 25 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,487; in 1841, 10,577. The town is situated on the canal d'Ourcq, 10 m. W of Meaux, at an alt. of 213 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,499. It contains some calico printing-mills and bleacheries. Lime and gypsum are wrought in the environs.

CLAYETTE (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, arrond. of Charolles. The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,242; in 1841, 13,126. The town is 12 m. S of Charolles. Pop. 1,221. It has some manufactories of calico and a considerable trade in cattle.
CLAYGATE, a manor in the p. of Thames-Ditton, Surrey. Pop. 940.
CLAYHANGER, a parish of Devon, 43 m. ENE of Bampton. Area 2,320 acres. Pop. 294.
CLAYHIDON, a parish of Devon, 134 m. ENE of Tiverton, S of the Black-Down hills. Area 4,530 acres. Pop. 849.
CLAYLANE, a township in the p. of North Wingfield, Derbyshire, 5½ m. S of Chesterfield. Pop. 1,478.
CLAYPOLE, a parish of Lincolnshire, 41 m. SE of Newark, on the Watham. Area 3,370 acres. Pop. 663.

CLAYSVILLE, a village of Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 219 m. W of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 292.—Also a village of Westland township, Guernsey co., in the state of Ohio, 87 m. E of Columbus, consisting of about 30 dwellings.
CLAYTHORPE, a chapelry in the p. of Belleau, Lincolnshire, 3 m. NW of Alford. Area 220 acres. Pop. 69.

CLAYTON, a township in the p. of Stoke-upon- Trent, Staffordshire, 2 m. SSE of Newcastle-under- Lyne, near the Manchester and Birmingham railway. Pop. 155.—Also a parish of Sussex, 12 m. E of Hurst-Pierpont, on the London and Brighton railway. Area 1,870 acres. Pop. 747.—Also a township in the p. and 31 m. SW of Bradford, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,610 acres. Pop. 4,347.

CLAYTON, a county of the state of Iowa, U.S., comprising an area of 720 sq. m., drained by Turkey river, which is navigable to steam-boats to the junction of the Volga river, Buck and Inymagill creeks, and Bloody-run and Yellow rivers. It contains some lead-mines. The soil on Turkey river is excellent. Pop. in 1840, 1,101. The cap. is Prairie la Porte.—Also a township of Jefferson co., in the state of New York, 12 m. NW of Watertown, bounded on the NW by the St. Lawrence, and drained by Chaumont river and French creek. Its surface is undulating, and its soil, consisting of sand and clay loam, is generally fertile. Pop. 2,990.—Also a village, cap. of Rabau co., in the state of Georgia, 181 m. N of Milledgeville, at the S base of the Blue ridge.—Also a village, cap. of Barbour co., in the state of Alabama, 179 m. SE of Tuscaloosa.—Also a township in the state of Ohio. Pop. 1,602.
CLAYTON-LE-DALE, a township in the p. and 4 m. NNW of Blackburn, Lancashire, E of the Ribble. Area 950 acres. Pop. 511.
CLAYTON-WITH-FRICKLEY, a parish of the W. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NW of Doncaster. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 316.
CLAYTON-GRIFFITH, a township in the p. of Trentham, Staffordshire. Area 590 acres. Pop. 56.
CLAYTON-LE-MOORS, a township in the p. of Whalley, Lancashire, 61 m. S of Clitheroe, intersected by the Leeds and Liverpool canal. Area 950 acres. Pop. 2,602.
CLAYTONVILLE, a village, cap. of Henderson co., in the state of North Carolina, 284 m. SW of Raleigh, on the N side of French Broad river.
CLAYTON (WEST), a township in the p. of High Hoyland, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. WNW of Barnesley. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 1,440.
CLAYTON-LE-WOODS, a township in the p. of Leyland, Lancashire, 33 m. NNW of Chorley, on the Wigan and Preston railway. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 795.
CLAYWORTH, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 5 m. NNE of East Retford, intersected by the Chesterfield canal. Area 3,190 acres. Pop. 627.
CLEADON, or CLEVEDON. See WHITBURN, Durham.
CLEAR (CAPE). See CAPE CLEAR.

CLEAR CREEK, a village of Ellington township, Chautauque co., in the state of New York, U. S., 319 m. SW of Albany. Pop. in 1840, about 250.—Also a township of Fairfield co., in the state of Ohio. Pop. 1,698.—Also a township of Warren co., in the same state. Pop. 2,882:—Also a township of Richland co., in the same state: Pop. 1,653.

CLEARFIELD, a county in the W part of the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., on the head-branches of the W fork of the Susquehanna. Area 1,425 sq. m. Pop. 7,834.—The cap., of the same name, is 126 m. NW of Harrisburg.—Also a township in Butler co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,113.—Also a township in Cambria co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 300.
CLEAR LAKE, or CLEAR-WATER LAKE, a small lake of North America, under the parallel of 55° 40', between Lake Buffalo on the NW, and Lake La Crosse on the SE. CLE
CLEAR SPRING, a village in Washington co., in the state of Maryland, U. S., 112 m. NW of Annapolis.—Also a township in Lagrange co., in Indiana. Pop. 226.—Also a v. in Kosciusko co., in Indiana.

CLEAR-WATER, or WASHACUMMU, a river of North America, which, flowing, in a valley about 3 m. broad, between ranges of hills from 600 to 1,000 ft. high, carries the waters of the Methye lake, nearly along the parallel of 56° 30', in a W course of 80 m., to the Athabasca or Elk river. At the junction of the two rivers, the Elk river is about 3 m. broad. The C. has numerous rapids and portages indicating a rapid descent towards the Athabasca. The hills, banks, and bed of the river, are composed of fine yellow sand with limestone rocks. The surface soil is alluvial.
CLEARWELL, a tything and chapelry in the p. of Newland, Gloucestershire, 7 m. W of Blakney. Pop. 674.
CLEASBY, a parish of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SW of Darlington, on the Tees, and on the North of England railroad. Area 970 acres. Pop. 188.

CLEATHAM, a township in the p. of Gainford, county of Durham, 41 m. ESE of Barnard-Castle. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 95.—Also a township in the p. of Manton, Lincolnshire, 6 m. SW of Glandford- Bridge. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 99.
CLEATHORPE, or CLEETHORPES, a township in the p. of Clee, Lincolnshire, 21 m. ESE of Great Grimsby. Pop. 778.
CLEATOR, or KEKELL-TERR, a parish of Cumberland, 2 m. N of Egremont, on the Eden. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. 763.
CLEAUGH, a village of co. Donegal, Ulster. Pop. in 1831, 102.
CLEAVERTON, a hamlet in the p. of Lea, Wilts. Pop. 116.

CLE'CY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Harcourt-Thury, near the I. bank of the Orne, 15 m. W of Falaise. Pop. 2,130. It has some manufactories of lace and cotton fabrics.
CLE'DEN-CAPSIZUN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Pont-Croix, 27 m. WNW of Quimper, at a short distance from the coast. Pop. 2,059.
CLE'DEN-POHER, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Carhaix, 22 m. E of Chateaulin. Pop. 1,397.
CLEDER, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Plouzevedé, 16 m. NW of Morlaix. Pop. in 1841, 5,002.
CLEE, a parish of Lincolnshire, 12 m. SE of Great Grimsby. Area 3,580 acres. Pop. 1,002.
CLEE (St. MARGARET'S), a parish of Salop, 175 m. NNE of Ludlow. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 269.
CLEER (St.), a parish of Cornwall, 23 m. N of Liskeard. Area 9,700 acres. Pop. 1,412.

CLEES, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district and 4 m. WSW of Orbe, on a narrow gorge of the Jura, and on the l. bank of the Orbe. Pop. 239. It was formerly a town and strong fortress, which was taken by assault and destroyed by the confederates in 1475.
CLEETON, a township in the p. of Cleeton, Salop. Pop. 70.
CLEEVE, a hamlet in the p. of Yatton, Somerset. Pop. 403.

CLEEVE-BISHOP'S, or BISHOP'S CLEEVE, a parish and township of Gloucestershire, 3 m. NNE of Cheltenham. Area 8,150 acres. Pop. 1,944; of township 682. On the ridge of hills called Cleeveclouds are the remains of ancient fortifications, and behind is Cleeve-cliff, in the Malvern range, which rises to the height of 630 ft.
CLEEVE (OLD), a parish of Somerset. Area Chapel Cleeve, which is 2 m. E of Dunsar. Its surface is covered with high craggy cliffs, which abound in alabaster.
CLEEVES-PRIOR, a parish of Worcestershire, 5 m. NE of Evesham, E of the Avon. Area 1,580 acres. Pop. 366.

CLEFMONT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, arrond. of Chaumont. The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,776; in 1841, 7,720. The town is 21 m. E of Chaumont. Pop. 512.
CLEFS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. and 7 m. N of Bauge. Pop. 1,208.
CLEGUER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Pont-Scorff, 8 m. N of Lorient. Pop. 2,120.

CLE'GUE'REC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of Pontivy. The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,715; in 1841, 13,291. The town is 7 m. NW of Pontivy. Pop. in 1841, 3,434.
CLEHONGER, a parish of Herefordshire. Area 640 acres. Pop. 171

CLEISH, a parish of Kinross-shire, comprising an area of 71 sq. m., and separated from the p. of Dunfermline by a range of hills of the same name, the highest point of which, Dumglow, rises to the height of 1,215 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 681.

CLELLES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, arrond. of Grenoble. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 4,028; in 1841, 4,151. The village is 28 m. S of Grenoble. Pop. in 1846, 746. It has an annual fair for sheep, goats, pigs, &c.
CLEMENT (SAINT), a parish of the island of Jersey. Pop. 1,491.

CLE'MENT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Alpes, cant. of Guillestre, 8 m. NE of Embrun, on the r. bank of the Durance. Pop. 611. It contains quarries of marble and porphyry, and a marble-work.—Also a commune in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Seilhac, 9 m. NNW of Tulle. Pop. 1,600.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Manche, cant. and 3 m. NE of Mortain. Pop. 1,423.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Meurthe, cant. and 9 m. from Luneville. Pop. 1,357.
CLE'MENT-DES-LEVE'ES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. and 8 m. NW of Saumur, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 1,909.
